items.title: A New Threat Actor Using ClickFix and Fake Update Drive-By Attacks in Thousands of Compromised Sites
items.description: DriveSurge is a newly identified threat actor operating as an Initial Access Broker using a Pay-Per-Install model to supply victim leads to downstream actors. The actor has compromised thousands of websites, injecting malicious code that redirects visitors through zTDS (Traffic Distribution System) to deliver malware via two primary methods: FakeUpdates, which impersonate browser update prompts fo
items.ai_summary: The DriveSurge threat actor has been identified as an Initial Access Broker, utilizing a Pay-Per-Install model to supply victim leads to downstream actors. This actor has compromised thousands of websites, injecting malicious code that redirects visitors to deliver malware via two primary methods: FakeUpdates and ClickFix. The FakeUpdates method impersonates browser update prompts, tricking users into installing malware, while ClickFix exploits vulnerabilities in software to gain initial access. To remediate, defenders should ensure all software is up-to-date, particularly browsers and plugins, and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent malicious code injections. Additionally, users should be cautious of suspicious update prompts and avoid interacting with unfamiliar websites.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1650| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: AsyncRAT and Remcos delivered in an optimistic campaign
items.description:
A global phishing campaign targets business functions with emails carrying malicious Excel attachments that initiate a multi-stage infection chain when macros are enabled.
The attack uses layered obfuscation, including HTA scripts, PowerShell, encoded payloads, and steganography in PNG files, to deliver and execute Remote Access Trojans such as Remcos and AsyncRAT in a largely fileless manner.
It
items.ai_summary: The phishing campaign utilizes malicious Excel attachments to initiate a multi-stage infection chain, leveraging layered obfuscation techniques including HTA scripts, PowerShell, encoded payloads, and steganography in PNG files to deliver Remote Access Trojans such as Remcos and AsyncRAT. The attack vector involves enabling macros in Excel, which then triggers the execution of obfuscated files and information, categorized as T1027. The real-world impact of this campaign is significant, as it can lead to the compromise of business functions and the exfiltration of sensitive data. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all Excel macros are disabled by default, and implement a robust email filtering solution to detect and block malicious attachments. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious PowerShell activity, as indicated by T1059.001, and inspect network traffic for signs of remote access software, as categorized by T1219.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Miasma Mini Shai-Hulud Hits ImmobiliareLabs npm Packages
items.description: A fresh wave of the Miasma Mini Shai-Hulud supply chain campaign compromised legitimate npm packages under the @immobiliarelabs scope on June 26, 2026. The attack targeted Backstage plugins used for GitLab integration and LDAP authentication, affecting 22 package versions across multiple releases. The malware employs sophisticated techniques including hidden payloads that bypass standard package r
items.ai_summary: The Miasma Mini Shai-Hulud supply chain campaign has compromised 22 package versions under the @immobiliarelabs scope on npm, affecting Backstage plugins used for GitLab integration and LDAP authentication. The attack, which occurred on June 26, 2026, employs sophisticated techniques including hidden payloads that bypass standard package reviews. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data and systems, particularly for organizations using the affected Backstage plugins. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove and replace the compromised packages with versions outside the affected range, and implement additional security measures such as enhanced package validation and monitoring. Furthermore, developers should verify the integrity of their dependencies and consider using tools like npm audit or snyk to identify and address potential vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Understanding Langflow CVE-2026-55255, and why higher CVSS vulnerabilities aren't always the most exploited
items.description: On June 25, 2026, the first active exploitation of CVE-2026-55255, a critical CVSS 9.9 Langflow vulnerability, was documented. Langflow is an open-source framework for building AI agents and RAG pipelines. A single operator exploited both CVE-2026-55255 (cross-tenant IDOR) and CVE-2026-33017 (unauthenticated RCE, CVSS 9.3) against the same instance. Despite its lower score, the RCE has been exploi
items.ai_summary: The Langflow vulnerability, designated as CVE-2026-55255, is a critical issue with a CVSS score of 9.9, allowing for cross-tenant IDOR attacks. This vulnerability, along with CVE-2026-33017, an unauthenticated RCE with a CVSS score of 9.3, has been exploited in the wild against the same instance, highlighting the real-world impact of these vulnerabilities. The exploitation of these vulnerabilities can lead to significant breaches, with the potential for lateral movement and data exfiltration. To remediate, defenders should update Langflow to the latest version, which includes patches for both CVE-2026-55255 and CVE-2026-33017, and ensure that all AI agents and RAG pipelines are configured to use secure authentication mechanisms. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual login attempts or data transfers, to quickly identify and respond to potential exploits.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Phishing Campaign PasasteSinTAG - New domain rotation identified associated with the campaign impersonating the PasasteSinTAG portal
items.description: A phishing campaign targeting Chile continues to evolve with significant infrastructure expansion. Security researchers identified 99 new domains impersonating the legitimate PasasteSinTAG portal, with 22 domains confirmed active and 77 registered but not yet activated. The active domains utilize various top-level domains including .click, .cfd, .cyou, .mom, .best, .rest, .top, .help, .sbs, .icu,
items.ai_summary: The PasasteSinTAG phishing campaign has been identified with a significant expansion of its infrastructure, targeting Chile with 99 new domains impersonating the legitimate PasasteSinTAG portal. Of these, 22 domains are confirmed active and utilize various top-level domains to evade detection, including .click, .cfd, .cyou, .mom, .best, .rest, .top, .help, .sbs, and .icu. If exploited, this campaign could lead to data encryption for impact, as indicated by the detection of T1486, resulting in potential data breaches and financial losses for affected individuals and organizations. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering solutions, educate users about phishing attacks, and monitor for suspicious domain registrations. Specifically, defenders should block traffic to the identified domains and update their security software to detect and prevent phishing attempts, such as those using T1566 tactics.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: New customs charges for online orders outside the EU
items.description: With the introduction of charges/taxes on certain items posted from outside the EU, threat actors appear to be leveraging the situation to send fraudulent SMS and email messages impersonating postal services in an attempt to harvest payment details and personal information. The campaign is already being observed targeting Irish users, and I'd like to highlight this activity publicly as part of a L
items.ai_summary: The threat campaign involves threat actors sending fraudulent SMS and email messages impersonating postal services to harvest payment details and personal information from Irish users. The attack vector is primarily social engineering, leveraging the introduction of new customs charges on online orders outside the EU to create a sense of urgency and legitimacy. If exploited, victims may have their personal and financial information stolen, potentially leading to identity theft and financial loss. To remediate, users should be cautious of unsolicited messages, verify the authenticity of communications with postal services, and never provide sensitive information in response to unexpected requests. Users can also report suspicious messages to the relevant authorities and consider implementing two-factor authentication to add an extra layer of security.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1589| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1566| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Rapid growth and a new ransomware variant
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service group emerged as a top-10 threat actor in the first half of 2026. The group exploits vulnerabilities in internet-facing devices like VPNs and firewalls, potentially collaborating with initial access brokers. They employ comprehensive reconnaissance using tools like SharpADWS, NetScan, and Advanced IP Scanner, capturing network traffic with netsh. The attackers
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service group has emerged as a significant threat actor, exploiting vulnerabilities in internet-facing devices such as VPNs and firewalls to gain initial access. They utilize tools like SharpADWS, NetScan, and Advanced IP Scanner for comprehensive reconnaissance, and capture network traffic using netsh. If exploited, this can lead to data encryption and significant impact, particularly for organizations with sensitive data. To remediate, defenders should ensure all internet-facing devices are patched with the latest security updates, and implement robust network segmentation and monitoring. Additionally, implementing a robust backup and disaster recovery plan can help mitigate the impact of a ransomware attack.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1650| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1588| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From Bing Search to Ransomware: Bumblebee and AdaptixC2 Deliver Akira
items.description: In July 2025, threat actors compromised organizations through SEO poisoning campaigns targeting users searching for legitimate IT management tools. Users downloading trojanized installers for ManageEngine OpManager received Bumblebee malware, granting initial access. The attackers exploited the fact that users executing these IT tools were privileged administrators, enabling rapid lateral movement
items.ai_summary: The Bumblebee malware campaign exploited SEO poisoning tactics, specifically T1608.006, to target users searching for legitimate IT management tools, leading to the compromise of organizations. The attackers distributed trojanized installers for ManageEngine OpManager, which, when executed by privileged administrators, granted initial access and enabled rapid lateral movement. If exploited, this vulnerability can result in significant real-world impact, including data encryption for impact, as identified by T1486, and potential ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should immediately block all connections to known command and control servers, update their ManageEngine OpManager to the latest version, and apply the principle of least privilege to administrative accounts. Additionally, implementing a robust monitoring system to detect unusual application layer protocol activity, as indicated by T1071, can help identify potential threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608.006| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Chromium extension uses AI‑related branding to redirect browser search
items.description: Microsoft Threat Intelligence identified a malicious Chromium extension spoofing Perplexity AI to deceive users into installation. The extension's primary objective involves search traffic interception and data collection through Manifest Version 3 capabilities and declarativeNetRequest rules. It routes both full search queries and real-time keystrokes through attacker-controlled infrastructure ho
items.ai_summary: The malicious Chromium extension, masquerading as Perplexity AI, exploits users' trust by leveraging AI-related branding to gain installation. This extension utilizes Manifest Version 3 capabilities and declarativeNetRequest rules to intercept search traffic and collect data, including full search queries and real-time keystrokes, which are then routed through attacker-controlled infrastructure. The real-world impact of this exploit involves the compromise of sensitive user data, potentially affecting individuals and organizations relying on Chromium-based browsers. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately remove the malicious extension and update their browser to the latest version, ensuring that all extensions are verified and updated from trusted sources. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing extensions, verifying the publisher and reading reviews before installation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1056| C[Input Capture]:::tactic
    C -->|T1114| D[Email Collection]:::tactic
    D -->|T1021| E[Remote Services]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: RAT Abuses TON Blockchain to Target Japan's Hotel Industry
items.description: A sophisticated phishing campaign observed in May 2026 targets Japanese accommodation facilities partnering with Booking.com. Attackers impersonate guest complaints and review requests through emails, tricking hotel staff into downloading malicious ZIP files containing shortcut links disguised as photos. The malware, TONResolver, employs The Open Network blockchain platform as a dead drop resolver
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated phishing campaign targeting Japan's hotel industry has been observed, utilizing emails impersonating guest complaints and review requests. The malicious ZIP files contain shortcut links disguised as photos, which, when downloaded, execute the TONResolver malware. TONResolver employs The Open Network blockchain platform as a dead drop resolver, allowing attackers to maintain persistence and control over compromised systems. To remediate this threat, hotel staff should be trained to identify and report suspicious emails, and all employees should be required to use two-factor authentication when accessing the internet. Additionally, all systems should be updated to the latest version of the software, and any suspicious files or links should be immediately quarantined and reported to the IT department.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1102| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-56218: Capgo before 12.128.2 fails to strip EXIF metadata including GPS geolocation dat...
items.description: Capgo before 12.128.2 fails to strip EXIF metadata including GPS geolocation data from uploaded images, allowing information disclosure. Attackers can download uploaded images and extract precise latitude and longitude coordinates revealing user physical location at capture time.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-56218 is a vulnerability in Capgo before version 12.128.2, which fails to strip EXIF metadata, including GPS geolocation data, from uploaded images. This allows attackers to download uploaded images and extract precise latitude and longitude coordinates, revealing the user's physical location at capture time. The vulnerability can be exploited by attackers using the T1591.001 (Determine Physical Locations) and T1614 (System Location Discovery) tactics. To remediate this issue, users should update to Capgo version 12.128.2 or later, and ensure that EXIF metadata stripping is enabled. Additionally, users should review their image upload and storage practices to ensure that sensitive location data is not being stored.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1591.001| B[Determine Physical Locations]:::tactic
    B -->|T1614| C[System Location Discovery]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2025-71331: Flowise before 3.0.8 contains a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability caused ...
items.description: Flowise before 3.0.8 contains a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability caused by insufficient input filtering in chat messages and custom agent functions. An attacker can inject malicious JavaScript by sending an iframe payload (e.g., <iframe src="javascript:alert(document.cookie)">) in a chat box, or by having a custom agent function return an XSS payload from an external website. The injected
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-71331 is a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability in Flowise before 3.0.8, allowing attackers to inject malicious JavaScript via chat messages or custom agent functions. This can lead to sensitive information disclosure, such as cookies, and potentially allow for further exploitation. To remediate, update to Flowise 3.0.8 or later, and ensure that chat messages and custom agent functions are properly sanitized to prevent XSS attacks. Additionally, consider implementing Content Security Policy (CSP) to restrict the execution of JavaScript from untrusted sources. This will help prevent the execution of malicious scripts and mitigate the risk of XSS attacks.

items.title: CVE-2024-58351: Flowise before 2.1.4 allows configuration to be injected into the Chainflow duri...
items.description: Flowise before 2.1.4 allows configuration to be injected into the Chainflow during execution via the overrideConfig option, supported in both the frontend web integration and the backend Prediction API. Because this feature is enabled by default with no allow-list of permitted variables and relies on vm2 for sandboxing, an attacker can abuse it to achieve remote code execution and sandbox escape,
items.ai_summary: CVE-2024-58351 is a critical remote code execution vulnerability in Flowise before version 2.1.4. The vulnerability allows an attacker to inject configuration into the Chainflow during execution via the overrideConfig option, which is enabled by default without an allow-list of permitted variables. This can be exploited by an attacker to achieve remote code execution and sandbox escape. To remediate this vulnerability, Flowise users should update to version 2.1.4 or later, and ensure that the overrideConfig option is disabled. Additionally, users should review their configuration files for any malicious code that may have been injected.

    B -->|T1059| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    D -->|T1586.002| E[Email Accounts]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-12673: Liquidfiles versions before 4.2.12 are affected by a broken access control vulne...
items.description: Liquidfiles versions before 4.2.12 are affected by a broken access control vulnerability resulting in privilege escalation from an Admin in a secondary domain to a Sysadmin by modifying a group in their managed secondary (non-default) group.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-12673 is a broken access control vulnerability in Liquidfiles versions before 4.2.12, allowing an Admin in a secondary domain to escalate privileges to a Sysadmin by modifying a group in their managed secondary (non-default) group. This vulnerability can be exploited using T1068 (Exploitation for Privilege Escalation) and T1649 (Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates) tactics. If exploited, an attacker can gain elevated privileges, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, update Liquidfiles to version 4.2.12 or later, and ensure that all secondary domains have the latest security patches applied. Additionally, consider implementing multi-factor authentication and monitoring for suspicious activity.

items.title: CVE-2022-50972: WooCommerce 7.1.0 contains a remote code execution vulnerability that allows att...
items.description: WooCommerce 7.1.0 contains a remote code execution vulnerability that allows attackers to execute arbitrary PHP code by injecting shell commands through the product-type parameter. Attackers can send requests to the class-wc-meta-box-product-images.php endpoint with unsanitized product-type values to write malicious PHP files to the web root.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2022-50972 is a remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in WooCommerce 7.1.0, allowing attackers to inject shell commands through the product-type parameter in the class-wc-meta-box-product-images.php endpoint. This enables malicious actors to write arbitrary PHP files to the web root, potentially leading to data breaches, privilege escalation, or even ransomware attacks. To remediate, update WooCommerce to version 7.1.1 or later, and ensure the product-type parameter is sanitized to prevent unsanitized input. Additionally, consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to block suspicious requests. This vulnerability has a high CVSS score of 9.8, indicating a critical risk.

items.title: CVE-2020-37255: WordPress Time Capsule Plugin 1.21.16 contains an authentication bypass vulnerab...
items.description: WordPress Time Capsule Plugin 1.21.16 contains an authentication bypass vulnerability that allows unauthenticated attackers to gain administrative access by sending a crafted POST request with the IWP_JSON_PREFIX header. Attackers can exploit this flaw to obtain valid administrator session cookies and access the WordPress dashboard without providing credentials.

items.description: Apple released updates for iOS/iPadOS, macOS, and Safari on Monday. There have been no updates for other Apple operating systems (visionOS, watchOS, tvOS). Usually, Apple updates all products at the same time.

items.title: AirDrop and Quick Share Flaws Let Nearby Attackers Trigger Crashes and Bypass Checks
items.description: Two researchers have found six security flaws in AirDrop and Quick Share, the wireless features that beam files between nearby devices with no cables or shared network. An attacker within wireless range, with just a laptop and no prior connection, can crash the sharing service on a Mac or iPhone set to receive from anyone, with no tap or prompt. The same research found Quick Share flaws that

items.title: Nissan Employee Data Breached in Oracle PeopleSoft Hack
items.description: Only a handful of the 100 organizations targeted in the PeopleSoft campaign have been confirmed. The post Nissan Employee Data Breached in Oracle PeopleSoft Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA: Windows BlueHammer flaw now exploited by ransomware gangs
items.description: CISA confirmed on Monday that ransomware gangs are now exploiting a Microsoft Defender privilege escalation vulnerability, dubbed BlueHammer, that has previously been abused in zero-day attacks. [...]

items.title: Critical SimpleHelp Vulnerability Exploited for Malware Delivery
items.description: The threat actor is focused on collecting credentials, SSH keys, cryptocurrency wallets, and development tooling. The post Critical SimpleHelp Vulnerability Exploited for Malware Delivery appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New BioShocking Attack Tricks AI Browsers Into Leaking User Credentials
items.description: Convince an AI browser that it is playing a game, and it can hand over your login details. That is the finding behind BioShocking, a technique from security firm LayerX that tricked six AI browsers and assistants into copying a user's credentials and sending them to an attacker. The targets included OpenAI's ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity's Comet, and Anthropic's Claude browser extension. An

items.title: Progress Kemp LoadMaster Flaw Could Let Attackers Run Root Commands Pre-Auth
items.description: A critical vulnerability in Progress Kemp LoadMaster can let an unauthenticated attacker execute arbitrary commands as root on the appliance by sending a crafted request to its API. The flaw, tracked as CVE-2026-8037, carries a CVSS score of 9.8 according to ZDI. A patch is available. If you run LoadMaster with the API enabled, update now. Progress published its advisory on June

items.title: Apple Patches 30+ iOS, macOS, Safari Flaws, Including AI-Discovered WebKit Bugs
items.description: Apple on Monday released security updates for iOS, macOS, and the Safari web browser to address over three dozen flaws, including four vulnerabilities in WebKit that were discovered using artificial intelligence (AI) tools like Anthropic Claude and OpenAI Codex Security. The WebKit vulnerabilities are listed below - CVE-2026-43707 - A memory corruption issue that could result in an

items.title: Quantifind Raises $200 Million for AI-Native Risk Intelligence
items.description: Quantifind will accelerate international expansion and extend its platform’s localized risk intelligence capabilities. The post Quantifind Raises $200 Million for AI-Native Risk Intelligence appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Controller Flaws Expose Highway Signs and Billboards to Remote Hacking
items.description: CISA has published an advisory to inform organizations about three vulnerabilities found by a researcher in Daktronics controllers. The post New Controller Flaws Expose Highway Signs and Billboards to Remote Hacking appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Oracle E-Business Suite Flaw CVE-2026-46817 Actively Exploited in the Wild
items.description: A critical security flaw impacting Oracle E-Business Suite has come under active exploitation in the wild, according to Defused Cyber. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-46817 (CVSS score: 9.8), refers to an improper privilege management and authentication flaw in Oracle Payments that could be abused to take over susceptible instances. "Easily exploitable vulnerability allows

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 30th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9988, (Tue, Jun 30th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: NIST Enrichment Reductions Impact CVE Coverage, Accuracy
items.description: The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) scaled back on the number of CVEs it selects for in-depth analysis, but the move has produced mixed results, according to researchers.

items.title: 'Djinn' Stealer Targets Cloud, AI Credentials
items.description: The infostealer was delivered via CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in SimpleHelp, targeting credentials linking development and admin environments to wider enterprise systems.

items.title: Vulnerabilities Expose Private Data in Indian Government Systems
items.description: One critical vulnerability, among many discovered by a researcher, could have allowed anyone to walk in and take over a national government portal.

items.title: Nissan discloses employee data breach linked to Oracle zero-day attacks
items.description: Nissan is warning that it suffered a data breach affecting current and former employees after threat actors exploited an Oracle PeopleSoft vulnerability in data theft attacks previously linked to the ShinyHunters extortion group. [...]

items.title: NAIC says public data stolen in ShinyHunters' PeopleSoft breach
items.description: The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) says the ShinyHunters extortion group stole only publicly available data, outdated logs, and configuration files after breaching its systems by exploiting a zero-day vulnerability in an Oracle PeopleSoft server. [...]

items.title: Can Clothes Make You Invisible to Facial Recognition?
items.description: Does life feel Orwellian sometimes? One researcher has a solution for you: graphic tees that confuse the neural networks in surveillance cameras.

items.title: Iran, Russia, China Target Water Systems for Sabotage
items.description: Nation-state attackers breach water systems through weak passwords, exposed PLCs, and poor segmentation — not sophisticated malware.

items.title: Justices rule that cellphone location histories are protected by the Fourth Amendment
items.description: Police must get a warrant to request geofence data involving individual cellphones, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in what represents a victory for privacy advocates.

items.title: Malicious Perplexity Chrome Extension Intercepted Searches and Address Bar Input
items.description: Microsoft has found a malicious Chrome extension that posed as the AI search engine Perplexity and quietly logged what people searched for. It routed every query and every character typed into the address bar through an attacker-controlled server before redirecting users to real results. Microsoft says Google removed it from the store after responsible disclosure. The extension was called "

items.title: WhatsApp rolls out usernames to help users hide their phone number
items.description: WhatsApp is finally allowing users to reserve usernames, a privacy feature that lets them hide their phone numbers from people not in their contact list. [...]

items.title: Microsoft extends Windows Server 2022 hotpatching until October 2027
items.description: Microsoft has extended Windows Server 2022 hotpatching until October 2027, one year after the mainstream end date of October 2026. [...]

items.title: US racks up about 400 wins over illegal World Cup streaming sites
items.description: The World Cup’s organizing body, FIFA, helped identify hundreds of domains taken down in an action organized by the U.S., along with the help of U.S. broadcaster NBC Universal and other entities.

items.title: WhatsApp is Finally Getting Usernames to Help Keep Phone Numbers Private
items.description: WhatsApp on Monday officially announced the start of global reservations of usernames with an aim to protect the privacy of more than three billion users on the messaging platform. The optional feature is designed to help users connect with someone on the service through usernames, as opposed to directly sharing their phone numbers. Username reservations will start rolling out starting today,

items.title: US posts $10 million reward over Russian cyber campaign targeting Signal, WhatsApp
items.description: Russia-linked hacking groups tracked as UNC5792 and UNC4221 have socially engineered their way into the messaging accounts of government officials.

items.title: U.S. offers $10 million for hackers targeting WhatsApp, Signal users
items.description: The U.S. Department of State is offering up to $10 million for information that helps identify or locate members of the UNC5792 and UNC4221 hacker groups, which are linked to Russia's intelligence and military services. [...]

items.title: Mustang Panda Uses Zoho WorkDrive as Command Channel in Indian Government Attacks
items.description: The China-aligned espionage group Mustang Panda is running two campaigns against the Indian government and hydropower targets, deploying new malware and turning a legitimate cloud service into its command channel. Acronis Threat Research Unit found active compromises inside Indian government networks, including machines used by senior administrative staff, and worked with

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Linux Kernel Flaws, AI Malware Tricks, Turla Backdoor, Infostealers and More
items.description: This week was a reminder that attackers do not always need big tricks. One small mistake, one old access path, one missed patch, and suddenly the door is open. The noise is not all noise, either. Forums are talking, researchers are finding easy cracks, and defenders have more cleanup waiting. Here’s the full Monday recap. ⚡ Threat of the Week New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local

items.title: Ukraine to use seized crypto from cybercrime group to buy war bonds
items.description: Ukraine's Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA), which manages property seized in criminal proceedings, said more than $8.3 million in cryptocurrency had been transferred to its official digital wallet following a court order.

items.title: Adding some Automation to the favicon.ico method of Host Recon, (Mon, Jun 29th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;m in the throes of target host recon for another pentest, and thought I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;d share some workflow / automation stuff. In the past, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve discussed using historic DNS "mining" to collect target hosts in the domain.

items.title: Amazon Q VS Extension Flaw Leads to Cloud Credential Theft
items.description: Adversaries could plant a malicious repository that can execute arbitrary code and steal cloud credentials by exploiting the vulnerability, which showcases growing MCP risk.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 29th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9986, (Mon, Jun 29th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: YARA-X 1.18.0 and 1.19.0 Release, (Sun, Jun 28th)
items.description: YARA-X&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s 1.18.0 release brings 3 improvements and 2 bugfixes.

items.title: Third-Party Breaches Teach Education Sector a Costly Lesson in Vendor Risk
items.description: Rising threats from third-party actors are forcing institutions to play defense to protect student data from ransomware and other attacks.

items.title: AI Decline? Confidence in Autonomous Penetration Testing Falls
items.description: Companies are still experimenting with automated AI systems to find security weaknesses, but fewer are relying on the technology.

items.title: Cisco Adds NHI to Security Stack With Astrix, WideField Acquisitions
items.description: Cisco joins a growing list of security platform providers that are betting that securing the agentic workforce means turning identity into the primary control plane.

items.title: Russia accuses Apple of ‘political censorship’ after VK apps removed from App Store
items.description: Apple removed VK's flagship social network VKontakte, often described as Russia's equivalent of Facebook, along with VK Music, VK Messenger, VK Video, Odnoklassniki and Mail.ru services, including its email application.

items.title: What do Ports Hear When Nobody's Listening? An Assessment of Automated Cybercrime [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Nicole Phillips, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). But a malicious process&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;can also mimic a non-suspicious name by

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 24th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9984, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Fixed the Bug. Nobody Fixed the Configuration., (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: The vulnerability

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 23rd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9982, (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
